hero.subtitle: Understand how technologies relate, the request flow, caching, and database access.

config.title: Configuration and Environment Variables
config.intro: All configuration is centralized through config files in src/config/ and accessed via ConfigService. Environment variables are loaded automatically.

items.app: app.config.ts - Application settings
items.database: database.config.ts - MongoDB and PostgreSQL (includes pool)
items.redis: redis.config.ts - Redis cache
items.kafka: kafka.config.ts - Kafka messaging
items.jwt: jwt.config.ts - JWT authentication
items.bcrypt: bcrypt.config.ts - Password hashing
items.smtp: smtp.config.ts - SMTP email
items.minio: minio.config.ts - MinIO storage
items.graylog: graylog.config.ts - Graylog logging (GELF)
items.whatsapp: whatsapp.config.ts - WhatsApp API

clientRequest.title: 1. Client sends HTTP request
clientRequest.description: Request arrives at the NestJS server with headers (Authorization, Content-Type, etc.)

securityMiddleware.title: 2. Security Middleware
securityMiddleware.description: SecurityModule intercepts and validates requests, applies rate limiting and basic protections

jwtAuth.title: 3. JWT Authentication
jwtAuth.description: JwtAuthGuard validates the JWT token. Routes marked with @Public() are skipped.

rolesAuth.title: 4. Role Authorization
rolesAuth.description: RolesGuard checks whether the user has the required roles defined via @Roles()

cacheInterceptor.title: 5. Cache Interceptor
cacheInterceptor.description: TypeOrmCacheInterceptor or MongooseCacheInterceptor checks for cached data in Redis

controller.title: 6. Controller/Resolver
controller.description: Request reaches the controller (REST) or resolver (GraphQL)

serviceLayer.title: 7. Service Layer
serviceLayer.description: Business logic runs in the service, which uses repositories for data access

repositoryPattern.title: 8. Repository Pattern
repositoryPattern.description: The module service injects the chosen repository — PostgreSQL (TypeORM) and/or MongoDB (Mongoose) — based on domain needs. Two repositories per domain allow a single database or CQRS (commands in one, queries in the other).

logging.title: 9. Logging (Graylog)
logging.description: Interceptor and exception filter send structured GELF logs to Graylog (when GRAYLOG_ENABLED=true).

responseCache.title: 10. Response and Cache
responseCache.description: Data is returned to the client and, when applicable, stored in Redis for subsequent requests

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.description: The cache system uses Redis as an intermediate layer to store results from frequent queries, reducing database load and improving response time.

flow.title: Cache Flow
steps: Interceptor checks if a key exists in Redis
If it exists: return cached data (cache hit)
If not: run the database query, store in Redis, and return (cache miss)
Data expires automatically after the configured TTL

templatePattern.title: Template pattern
templatePattern.description: The template keeps MongoDB and PostgreSQL in the stack. Each domain can have two repositories; the service chooses one database or both (CQRS). There is no mandatory command/query split — the choice is per module.

connectionPools.title: Connection pools
connectionPools.description: Both connections use an explicit pool (configured in database.config.ts). The GET /health endpoint includes pool metadata in the postgres and mongo checks.

postgres.title: PostgreSQL
items: Use as the single source of truth or as the query side in CQRS

mongo.title: MongoDB
items: Use alone or as the command side in CQRS

cqrsExample.title: Example: enable CQRS
cqrsExample.description: Inject both repositories in the service: commands in one database, queries in the other. Optionally sync with Kafka for eventual consistency.

alert.title: Mongo or Postgres
alert.description: You can use Postgres only, Mongo only, or both (CQRS). The choice is in the service/module without removing the other database infrastructure from the project.

items.nestjsDatabases: NestJS ↔ MongoDB/PostgreSQL: The framework communicates with databases through Mongoose and TypeORM; the service chooses which repository to use
items.nestjsRedis: NestJS ↔ Redis: Cache interceptors use Redis to store results
items.nestjsKafka: NestJS ↔ Kafka: Events and messages are published/consumed via Kafka (useful for projecting data in CQRS)
items.nestjsMinio: NestJS ↔ MinIO: File upload and download via S3-compatible API
items.nestjsGraylog: NestJS ↔ Graylog: Structured logs via GELF HTTP (interceptor + exception filter)
items.mongoPostgres: MongoDB ↔ PostgreSQL (optional): In a CQRS design, synchronization via Kafka events or async processes

samples.publishKafkaEvent: // publish Kafka event to project into Postgres...
